topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was thick, cream-colored, bearing a seal that felt impossibly formal. It arrived on a Tuesday, completely unannounced, and the sheer audacity of it made my jaw tighten. I hadn't expected anything from him, not even a ghost of contact. When I finally tore it open, the paper felt brittle under my fingertips. Inside, the script was neat, almost painfully polite. It detailed his regret, his acknowledgment of the damage, and a lengthy explanation of his remorse. I read it once, twice, then crumpled the paper into a tight, furious ball. The sound of the crumpling was loud in the quiet apartment. I threw the wad against the wall, watching the scraps flutter down like discarded ash. My hands were shaking, a low, simmering heat rising in my chest that I desperately tried to smother with shallow breaths. It was a waste of my time, a pathetic attempt at reconciliation from someone who clearly hadn't learned anything.

[story 2]

I found the letter tucked inside a forgotten book, a relic from a time I’d tried to bury. The ink was faded, the handwriting shaky, as if the writer was struggling to form the words. When I finally deciphered the contents, a cold, hard knot formed in my stomach. It was an apology, years late, for something that had irrevocably shifted the landscape of my life. I didn't even bother to read the closing sentiments; the sheer effort it took to process the admission felt exhausting. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room until the floorboards creaked under my weight. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the desk. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, feeling a profound sense of dismissal wash over me. The paper felt like an insult, a flimsy piece of paper trying to mend something that was fundamentally broken.

[story 3]

It landed on my doorstep with a dull thud, startling me out of a deep, uneasy sleep. I didn't recognize the handwriting immediately; it was stylized, almost theatrical. When I finally managed to unfold the parchment, the words swam before my eyes, each sentence laced with an excessive degree of contrition. A slow, deliberate sigh escaped my lips, a sound devoid of warmth. I didn't need to read the specifics; the tone was painfully self-absorbed. I picked up a heavy glass and turned it over and over in my hands, the cold glass offering a momentary, sharp distraction from the churning resentment inside me. I set it down with a decisive clink. This gesture, this belated acknowledgment, only served to sharpen the edges of my displeasure. I took a deep, deliberate breath, the air catching painfully in my throat, and then I simply walked away from the door, closing it softly behind me, sealing the unwelcome message inside.
